Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: No it would not be a good idea since its Tg temperature is low. It will be soft at high temperatures and it will deform meaning that it will not be useful for frying eggs. A property that should be desired is a polymer with a higher Tg.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: During stress relaxation, the tensile stress required to hold the material at the current deformation decreases over time as the polymer structure adjusts and moves around to distribute the load. As the material is held at the current strain, the elastic recovery decreases and therefore the change in load is greater in the beginning of the test.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: The drop to 37% of the initial stress is relative to the initial stress value. Further along in the process, the time to 37% remains constant, so the drop in that time must be less since the value has already been reduced. It is a matter of exponential decay, similar to the concept of the half-life of radioactive isotopes.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: The glass transition and the melting temperature are important components in design. Polymers should be ridged (if intended for cooking) for beyond the temperatures they can be exposed to. Spatulas should probably have a TG of grater than 100* c.

standard_answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!). The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

In the case of 60% reduction in area there is already a lot of plastic deformation, recrystallization, and grain deformation that has occurred in the specimen. Thus, the material starts to recrystallize at much lower energy to reach a lower energy state.

Question: Why do we use a Weibull distribution for the chalk samples instead of normal distribution? (Explain how the material and manufacturing affect it)
Expected Answer: Industrially manufactured chalks have high variability in their material properties. The final product ends up with many internal flaws. “The Weibull distribution is an indicator of the variability of strength of materials resulting from a distribution of flaw sizes. This behavior results from the critical sized flaws in materials with distribution of flaw size (i.e., failure due to the weakest link in a chain).Moreover, the strength has the known threshold strength of 0 MPa. A normal distribution of such high variability data and known threshold will predict possibility of failure strengths below zero MPa.
student_answer: A weibull distribution models skewed data more accurately. For a manufactured material, a normal distribution is not necessarily how failure over time would be distributed. Depending on the treatments applied to a material (hot/cold working, etc) a material could be significantly skewed left or right for failure. An extremely brittle and weak material such as chalk would be skewed differently to a much stronger and ductile material such as steel as well.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: No, PLA would not be a good material for a flexible spatula. The average stovetop temperature for medium heat is at around 177 degrees Celsius, and although the PLA would be flexible since the operating temperature is above the glass transition temperature, PLA melts at 170-180 degrees Celsius. In terms of frying eggs, maybe PLA would work, but the temperature of frying eggs is 70 celsius, which is super close to the glass transition temperature. You would also have to heat up the material above the glass transition temperature. It's recommended to find another polymer with a higher glass transition temperature. The other property that one would have to consider besides the glass transition temperature is the melting temperature.

A four-point bend test is also a pure bending test. There is no shear force between the internal loading points and therefore the only load applied is a bending moment. Question: Are polymers elastic, viscoelastic or viscous? What does this mean?
Expected Answer: Polymers are a viscoelastic material. They exhibit both elastic (solid like) and viscous (fluid like) behavior when undergoing deformation at different temperatures and conditions.
student_answer: Depending on their structure, composition, and processing conditions, polymers can exhibit elastic, viscoelastic, or viscous behavior. When stressed, elastic polymers deform but return to their original shape when the stress is removed. Viscoelastic polymers exhibit both elastic and viscous behavior, as evidenced by a nonlinear stress-strain relationship, with stress and strain varying with deformation rate and time. When stressed, viscous polymers behave like liquids and deform continuously, with no significant elastic behavior, as defined by a linear or nonlinear stress-strain relationship. Understanding polymer mechanical behavior is critical for designing and optimizing polymer-based materials for specific applications, and polymer behavior is affected by a variety of factors, including temperature, strain rate, and the presence of additives or fillers.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: The change in load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes because the chains of the polymers are stretching out and aligning, but after the two minutes they are more aligned already so the change is smaller.

Question: Would you recommend using a 4-point bending test and why (only one answer required)?
Expected Answer: a. It is sometime recommended to use a 4-point bending test since the maximum moment is the same between the two internal loading points which in turn will minimize the variability in failure strength as now we look for the critical crack in a region then a point for three-point bending.b.  A four-point bend test is also a pure bending test. There is no shear force between the internal loading points and therefore the only load applied is a bending moment. This isolates the effect of the bending moment instead of having he combined loading in the 3-point bending test
student_answer: A 4 point bend test should be utilized when the material being tested is non-homogenous. An example of this is when a composite material is being tested. This is because during a four point test, the induced stress is less concentrated and spread out over a larger area of the material. This helps to avoid premature failure in composite materials. Essenially, a larger portion of the material is subjected to a bending moment which propagates the possibility for material properties to confound results.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Therefore, given that it is exponential decay, the slope of the curve is steepest at the beginning. Because the slope in this case correlates to the stress relaxation rate, the change in the load is greatest during the beginning of the two minutes.

This makes it a more flexible and versatile tool for analyzing failure data, particularly for materials that are subject to a range of different failure modes.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: Yes, this would be a good idea, as long as the cooking temperature is below PLA's melting temperature. The visco-elastic region between glass-transition and melting point would be suitable for cooking and having flex in the spatula.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?

Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: This is a bad idea. The heat from the stove would reach the Tg much faster and cause the spatula to melt. A polymer with a very high Tg would be desired because it is much less likely to melt as the heat from the stove will not likely exceed the polymer's Tg.

student_answer: We use a weibull distribution for the chalk samples instead of a normal distribution because the strength of brittle materials is not perfectly gaussian. Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: The change in strain rate is at its maximum at the beginning of the two minutes--the rate is suddenly held constant/rate of change of the strain rate goes abruptly to zero. It is at this period of maximum change in strain rate that maximum initial stress relief would occur (conversion of elastic to plastic strain) and thereby decrease the stress ("load") experienced by the polymer material by said 37% (the time constant, if under two minutes, is reached and thus 37% of initial stress has been reduced; stress relaxation tends to taper off and approach a final value, and so the greatest change in load would be experienced at the onset of stress relaxation).

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: Stress relaxation of polymers under fixed strain is determined by the relaxation time constant, which defines the rate at which stress decreases. Initially, stress is relatively high, resulting in a higher force or load exerted by the polymer. As time passes, the relaxation time constant takes effect, causing stress and the corresponding force or load to decrease.

In the case of 60% reduction in area there is already a lot of plastic deformation, recrystallization, and grain deformation that has occurred in the specimen. Thus, the material starts to recrystallize at much lower energy to reach a lower energy state.

The activation energy with 30% reduction will be larger than that at the 60% reduction.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: At the beginning of the relaxation period, there is greater stress being applied to the material. As the material expands, this stress is reduced, causing there to be less expansion until there isn't enough stress to continue to deform the material.

Question: Are polymers elastic, viscoelastic or viscous? What does this mean?
Expected Answer: Polymers are a viscoelastic material. They exhibit both elastic (solid like) and viscous (fluid like) behavior when undergoing deformation at different temperatures and conditions.
student_answer: While some polymers are viscoelastic (encompassing both elastic and viscous), not all are either elastic or viscous. Elastic means that the polymer will recover all of its deformation after strain (no plastic deformation), while viscous means the opposite. This is heavily affected by strain rate, where a polymer strained at low strain rates will show elastic behavior and one strained at high strain rates will show viscous behavior. This is referred to viscoelasticity. Some polymers like elastomers exhibit really elastic behavior where they can experience over 200% elastic deformation. Other polymers like thermosets will experience more viscous and brittle behavior.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: When a polymer is put under tensile stress, the polymer chains untangle and the material elongates. However, it is harder to untangle the chains initially. Over time, it is easier to stretch the material than it was originally. This is why we see a greater load at the beginning.

Question: Would you recommend using a 4-point bending test and why (only one answer required)?
Expected Answer: a. It is sometime recommended to use a 4-point bending test since the maximum moment is the same between the two internal loading points which in turn will minimize the variability in failure strength as now we look for the critical crack in a region then a point for three-point bending.b.  A four-point bend test is also a pure bending test. There is no shear force between the internal loading points and therefore the only load applied is a bending moment. This isolates the effect of the bending moment instead of having he combined loading in the 3-point bending test
student_answer: I would recommend using a 4-point bending test when a brittle material can not withstand shear stress very well. For a 3-point bending test, there are shear stresses applied on either side of the applied load, all the way to the loading pins. However, for a 4-point bending test, the shear stresses from the loading pin to the applied load is very small, with no shear stresses between the two loading pins. Therefore, if a material can not withstand shear stresses, then a 4-point bending test would be better. Additionally, a 4-point bending test is more stable and prevents shifting with 2 applied loads, which can reduce error and local drops.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?

Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

When choosing another polymer, a higher Tg than the applied heat of the material's intended application would be desired.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: Would you recommend using a 4-point bending test and why (only one answer required)?
Expected Answer: a. It is sometime recommended to use a 4-point bending test since the maximum moment is the same between the two internal loading points which in turn will minimize the variability in failure strength as now we look for the critical crack in a region then a point for three-point bending.b.  A four-point bend test is also a pure bending test. There is no shear force between the internal loading points and therefore the only load applied is a bending moment. This isolates the effect of the bending moment instead of having he combined loading in the 3-point bending test
student_answer:
4-point bending is best used in materials that are brittle, small, anisotropic, or thin/flexible.

Brittle - Premature failure can be reduced, as bending is induced rather than direct tensile stress.

Small - This test provides a more uniform stress distribution than other tests.

Anisotropic - Some materials have different mechanical properties in different orientations. Four point bending can test in different orientations

Thin-flexible - This test allows for both tensile and compressive stresses, allowing for more accuracy.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: At rest, the molecules within the polymers are held in place by both the primary and secondary bonds between the molecules. When a load is applied, those bonds end up taking a large amount of stress, causing a large amount of bond breakage. As the bonds between molecules break, the more the molecules are allowed to stretch and align in the direction the load direction. The more the molecules stretch the less stress is placed on the intermolecular bonds until the stretched molecules and the bonds are able to withstand the load/breakage. In short, load breaks the intermolecular bonds at once, forcing the molecules to stretch to make up for it. Since that process is not instantaneous, it takes a little time for that stress redistribution to happen and thus the load change is greater at the beginning.

$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

As these polymer chains find the most efficient way by which to bear the fixed strain, the stress begins to decrease drastically. After two minutes, the polymer chains generally have found the relaxed state/position that provides the most effective way to bear the stress the strain. Therefore, after two minutes, it is not expected to see significant change in the stress load.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: No, this is not a good idea because this means that the spatula would become rubbery or leathery anytime that it was used in conjunction with heat, which would make it fairly useless as a spatula. If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: Stress Relaxation is modeled by an exponential function, so at the beginning of the hold, the rate of stress relaxation is much much higher than near the end of the two minutes, because the curve is flattening off as it is nearing the 37% mark.

Expected Answer: Industrially manufactured chalks have high variability in their material properties. The final product ends up with many internal flaws. “The Weibull distribution is an indicator of the variability of strength of materials resulting from a distribution of flaw sizes. This behavior results from the critical sized flaws in materials with distribution of flaw size (i.e., failure due to the weakest link in a chain).Moreover, the strength has the known threshold strength of 0 MPa. A normal distribution of such high variability data and known threshold will predict possibility of failure strengths below zero MPa.
student_answer: Weibull distribution is commonly used to assess product reliability because, unlike the normal distribution, it can also model skewed data. Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

This is because when cooking the material will reach temperatures higher than the glass transition temp meaning it will become flimsy or even permanently deform while cooking. Instead, we should look for a polymer with a very high glass transition temperature

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: Stress relaxation can be described as the phenomenon that occurs when polymers relieve stress while being under constant strain. Therefore as time passes the stress will decrease. At the beginning of the two minutes, the change goes from 0-max, and then starts to trickle down after that because of said stress relaxation. This is why the most change occurs at the beginning of the applying of the constant strain.
